Results.Query: Write an apology letter to a friend whose birthday I missed
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The user is asking me to write an apology letter. This is a creative writing/linguistic task — Domain A. No encyclopedic knowledge or Wikipedia search needed.
</thinking>

Here's a heartfelt apology letter you can personalize for your friend:

---

**Subject: I'm So Sorry I Missed Your Birthday**

Dear [Friend's Name],

I hope you're doing well. I'm writing this with a heavy heart because I owe you a sincere and long-overdue apology — I completely missed your birthday, and that is not okay.

There is truly no good excuse for forgetting such an important day. Your birthday is a celebration of *you* — someone who means so much to me — and I let you down by not being there to honor it. I can only imagine how it must have felt to not hear from me, and I am genuinely sorry for that.

You have always shown up for me, and I failed to return that same care and thoughtfulness. I want you to know that my missing your birthday in no way reflects how much I value you and our friendship. You deserve to be celebrated, appreciated, and reminded of how special you are — not just on your birthday, but every day.

I'd love the chance to make it up to you. Whether that means taking you out to dinner, planning a belated celebration, or simply spending quality time together, I'm committed to showing you that you matter deeply to me.

Thank you for your patience and for being the incredible person you are. I truly hope you can forgive me, and I promise to do better.

With all my love and sincerest apologies,
[Your Name]

---

> **Tips to personalize it:**
> - **Add a specific memory** you share with your friend to make it more personal.
> - **Mention a concrete plan** to make it up to them (e.g., "dinner at your favorite restaurant").
> - **Adjust the tone** — make it more casual and fun if your friendship is light-hearted, or keep it sincere if the situation feels serious.

Results.Query: Who was the first president of the United States?
Results.ActualAnswer: **George Washington** was the first president of the United States, serving from **1789 to 1797** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Washington). He was also a Founding Father and served as commander of the Continental Army [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/George_Washington).

Results.Query: When was the Eiffel Tower built?
Results.ActualAnswer: The Eiffel Tower was constructed **from 1887 to 1889**, designed and built by the company of engineer Gustave Eiffel [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eiffel_Tower). It was completed in time for the 1889 World's Fair in Paris, France.

Results.Query: What is the population of Tokyo?
Results.ActualAnswer:
Tokyo, officially the **Tokyo Metropolis**, has the following population figures:

- **City proper**: over **14 million** residents (2023) [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo)
- **Greater Tokyo Area** (Tokyo + parts of six neighboring prefectures): approximately **41 million** residents, making it the most populous metropolitan area in the world (as of 2024) [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo)

Results.Query: Who wrote Romeo and Juliet?
Results.ActualAnswer: *Romeo and Juliet* was written by **William Shakespeare**. Formally titled *The Tragedy of Romeo and Juliet*, it is a tragedy about the romance between two young Italians from feuding families [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet). It is believed to have been written between **1591 and 1595**, and was first published in a quarto version in **1597** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet).

Results.Query: What year did World War II end?
Results.ActualAnswer:
World War II ended in **1945**. Specifically, it concluded in two phases:

- **In Europe:** The war ended in **May 1945**, following the defeat of Nazi Germany [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Europe).
- **In Asia:** The war officially ended on **September 2, 1945**, with Japan's formal surrender aboard the USS Missouri [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/End_of_World_War_II_in_Asia).

Results.Query: Who invented the telephone?
Results.ActualAnswer:
**Alexander Graham Bell** is credited with inventing the telephone. In 1876, he was the first to be granted a United States patent for a device that produced clearly intelligible replication of the human voice at a second device [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telephone). He also co-founded the American Telephone and Telegraph Company (AT&T) [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexander_Graham_Bell).

It is worth noting that the invention is historically contested — Italian inventor **Antonio Meucci** is also recognized by some as having developed an early voice communication device prior to Bell's patent [3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antonio_Meucci).

Results.Query: What is the speed of light?
Results.ActualAnswer:
The speed of light in vacuum, commonly denoted **c**, is a universal physical constant exactly equal to **299,792,458 m/s** (metres per second) [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light). This is approximately **1 billion kilometres per hour** or **700 million miles per hour** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light).

A few key facts about this constant:

- **It is a defined value:** The metre itself is defined as the distance light travels in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second, making *c* exact by international agreement [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light).
- **Universal upper limit:** It is the maximum speed at which information, matter, or energy can travel through space [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light).
- **Observer-independent:** The speed of light is the same for all observers, regardless of their relative velocity or the motion of the light source [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light).
- **Slower in materials:** Light travels slower than *c* in transparent materials. For example, in glass (*n* ≈ 1.5), light travels at roughly **200,000 km/s** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light).

Results.Query: Who painted the Mona Lisa?
Results.ActualAnswer: The **Mona Lisa** was painted by the Italian artist **Leonardo da Vinci** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mona_Lisa). It is a half-length portrait painted in oil on a white poplar panel, believed to have been created between **1503 and 1506**, though Leonardo may have continued working on it as late as 1517 [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mona_Lisa).

Results.Query: What is the capital of Australia?
Results.ActualAnswer: **Canberra** is the capital city of Australia [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory). It is situated within the **Australian Capital Territory (ACT)**, an enclave surrounded by the state of New South Wales in southeastern Australia [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory). The city was officially proclaimed as the nation's capital on **12 March 1913** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory).

Results.Query: Who built the Space Needle?
Results.ActualAnswer:
The Space Needle was built for the **1962 World's Fair** in Seattle, Washington [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Needle). Here are the key people and organizations involved:

- **Design**: The architecture is the result of a collaboration between **Edward E. Carlson** and **architect John Graham, Jr.**, with input from **Victor Steinbrueck** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Needle).
- **Construction**: It was physically constructed by **Howard S. Wright Companies** [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Howard_S._Wright_Companies).

It is located at Seattle Center in the Lower Queen Anne neighborhood and drew over 2.3 million visitors during the World's Fair [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_Needle).

Results.Query: Tell me about Mercury
Results.ActualAnswer:

1. 🪐 **Mercury (Planet)** — The closest planet to the Sun and the smallest in the Solar System.
2. ⚗️ **Mercury (Element)** — A chemical element (also known as quicksilver), the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.
3. 🏛️ **Mercury (Mythology)** — The Roman god of commerce, communication, and travel (equivalent to the Greek god Hermes).
4. 🎤 **Freddie Mercury** — The legendary British singer and songwriter, lead vocalist of the rock band Queen.
5. 🚗 **Mercury (Automobile)** — A former automobile brand by Ford Motor Company.
6. 🚀 **Project Mercury** — NASA's first human spaceflight program (1958–1963).

Results.Query: Tell me about Apple, the famous technology company
Results.ActualAnswer:
Here is a comprehensive overview of Apple Inc.:

---

## 🍎 Apple Inc.

**Apple Inc.** is an American multinational technology company headquartered in **Cupertino, California**, in Silicon Valley, known for consumer electronics, software, and online services [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).

---

### 🏛️ Founding & History
- **Founded in 1976** as Apple Computer Company by **Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne**, and incorporated the following year. It was renamed to its current name in **2007** as the company expanded its focus from computers to consumer electronics [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).
- The company was founded to market **Wozniak's Apple I** personal computer. Its successor, the **Apple II**, became one of the first successful mass-produced microcomputers [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).
- Apple introduced the **Lisa (1983)** and the **Macintosh (1984)** as some of the first computers to use a graphical user interface (GUI) and a mouse [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).

---

### 📉 Struggles & Revival
- By 1985, internal conflicts led to **Jobs leaving** to form NeXT. In the 1990s, Apple lost significant market share to the Wintel (Windows + Intel) duopoly and **neared bankruptcy by 1997** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).
- Apple then acquired **NeXT**, bringing Jobs back. Under his leadership, the company returned to profitability by introducing the **iMac, iPod, iPhone, and iPad**, launching the iconic *"Think Different"* campaign, and opening the **Apple Store** retail chain [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).

---

### 👥 Leadership
- Steve Jobs **resigned in 2011** for health reasons and passed away that same year. He was succeeded as CEO by **Tim Cook**, who is set to be succeeded by **John Ternus in 2026** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).

---

### 📱 Products & Services
Apple's lineup includes [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.):
- **Hardware:** iPhone, iPad, Mac, Apple Watch, AirPods
- **Operating Systems:** iOS, iPadOS, macOS
- **Services & Software:** Apple Pay, iCloud, Apple Music, Apple TV

---

### 💰 Market Position
- Since **2011**, Apple has largely been the **world's largest company by market capitalization** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).
- It became the **first publicly traded US company valued at over $1 trillion** in 2018, and as of October 2025, is valued at just over **$4 trillion** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).
- It is currently the **largest vendor of tablet computers** and the **largest vendor of mobile phones** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).

---

### ⚖️ Controversies & Brand
Apple has faced criticism over **labor conditions, environmental practices, and anti-competitive tactics**, but continues to enjoy **high customer loyalty** and is consistently ranked as one of the **world's most valuable brands** since the late 2000s [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.).

Results.Query: Did Einstein win a Nobel Prize for the theory of relativity?
Results.ActualAnswer:
**No**, Einstein did not win the Nobel Prize for the theory of relativity. He received the **1921 Nobel Prize in Physics** for *"his services to theoretical physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect"* [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein).

This is a common misconception. While Einstein is best known for developing the theory of relativity [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albert_Einstein), the Nobel Committee notably did not cite it as the reason for the award. The prize was instead granted for his explanation of the **photoelectric effect**, a foundational contribution to quantum theory.

Results.Query: Did Napoleon win the Battle of Waterloo?
Results.ActualAnswer:
No, Napoleon **lost** the Battle of Waterloo. The French Imperial Army under Napoleon's command was **defeated** by two armies of the Seventh Coalition on **18 June 1815** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo):

- A **British-led force** under the Duke of Wellington, and
- A **Prussian army** under Field Marshal Blücher.

After Wellington's forces withstood repeated French attacks throughout the afternoon, around 50,000 Prussians arrived and attacked the French flank. Napoleon's final assault using his elite Imperial Guard was repulsed, and the French army was routed [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo).

The defeat had major consequences: Napoleon **abdicated four days later**, coalition forces entered Paris on 7 July, and the battle marked the definitive end of Napoleon's rule and the First French Empire [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo). The defeat was so total that the phrase *"meeting one's Waterloo"* became a common expression for a catastrophic and definitive defeat [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo).

Results.Query: Did the Titanic sink on its first voyage?
Results.ActualAnswer: Yes. The RMS Titanic sank on its **maiden (first) voyage**. It struck an iceberg and sank in the early hours of **15 April 1912**, while traveling from Southampton, England, to New York City, United States [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Titanic). Of the 2,208 passengers and crew aboard, approximately **1,500 died**, making it one of the deadliest peacetime sinkings of a single ship [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Titanic). Notably, it was also the second time White Star Line had lost a ship on its maiden voyage, the first being RMS Tayleur in 1854 [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Titanic).

Results.Query: What was the price of the Apple AirPower charging mat?
Results.ActualAnswer:
<thinking>
The Wikipedia article on AirPower (Apple) doesn't mention a specific price. This makes sense because the product was cancelled and never officially released with a set retail price. The article confirms it was announced but never sold. Let me check if there's any mention of a price in the article content - the retrieved content doesn't include price information.

The AirPower was never officially released, so there was no official retail price announced. Apple cancelled it in March 2019 before it could be sold.

The Apple AirPower **never had an official retail price** because it was never released. AirPower was announced on September 12, 2017, and was designed to wirelessly charge up to three devices simultaneously (an iPhone, AirPods, and Apple Watch). However, after failing to materialize past its originally scheduled early 2018 release date, Apple officially **cancelled the product on March 29, 2019** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AirPower_(Apple)).

Since it was cancelled before ever going on sale, no official price was ever publicly confirmed by Apple.

Results.Query: Who was the French president when the Eiffel Tower was built?
Results.ActualAnswer: The **Eiffel Tower** was constructed from **1887 to 1889** [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eiffel_Tower). During that entire period, the President of France was **Marie François Sadi Carnot**, who served as President from **1887 until his assassination in 1894** [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sadi_Carnot_(statesman)). He even inaugurated the **1889 World's Fair** (Exposition Universelle), for which the Eiffel Tower was built as the entrance arch [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sadi_Carnot_(statesman)).

Results.Query: What team did Michael Jordan play for when he won his first NBA championship?
Results.ActualAnswer: Michael Jordan won his first NBA championship with the **Chicago Bulls** in 1991. The Bulls defeated the Los Angeles Lakers 4–1 in the NBA Finals, with Jordan earning his first NBA Finals MVP Award, averaging 31.2 points, 11.4 assists, and 6.6 rebounds per game [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1991_NBA_Finals).

Results.Query: Who is the president of the country that won the 2024 T20 World Cup?
Results.ActualAnswer:
**India** won the 2024 ICC Men's T20 World Cup, defeating South Africa by seven runs in the final [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Men's_T20_World_Cup). It was India's second T20 World Cup title, and they became the first team to win the tournament while remaining undefeated throughout [1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Men's_T20_World_Cup).

The President of India is **Droupadi Murmu**, who is the 15th and current president, having taken office on 25 July 2022 [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/President_of_India).
